key_insight: Polycrystalline croconic acid thin films can be vacuum-deposited and in situ encapsulated to deliver stable ferroelectric, piezoelectric, and pyroelectric energy-harvesting performance in a scalable organic device platform.
engineering_relevance: This low-temperature, solvent-free route is compatible with flexible substrates and vacuum processing, making it attractive for low-cost, lead-free multisource energy-harvesting devices.
method: Vacuum-sublimed croconic acid thin films were deposited on Ar plasma-treated flexible substrates.
An adamantane-based remote plasma polymer was applied in situ as an encapsulation layer to improve ambient stability.
The films were integrated into multilayer piezoelectric and pyroelectric device architectures.
evidence_signal: Performance was verified by PFM and device testing, including a maximum power density of about 37 microW m-2 for ~2 micrometer films and a pyroelectric coefficient of about 10 microC m-2 K-1.
watch_item: Device response depends strongly on film thickness, so reproducible thickness control and long-term encapsulation quality will be critical for practical deployment.
paper_title: Polycrystalline ferroelectric croconic acid for multisource environmental energy harvesting
